topic: Sharp identification of the long-run average treatment effect in a discrete-time panel where treatment is reversible (non-absorbing) and dynamic spillovers between consecutive treatment spells are bounded by an explicit scalar parameter: a closed-form sequence of nested g-formulas characterizing the identified set as a function of the spillover bound, recovering the Robins (1986) g-formula point identification when the bound is zero and the unrestricted Manski-style bounds when the bound is infinite, with a sharpness witness in the form of two extremal joint laws that saturate each face of the identified set; extends Han-Yao-Hsu (2024) absorbing-treatment dynamic identification to the reversible regime explicitly left open in their conclusion

open_problems.open_problem: Give sharp scalar-indexed bounds for the long-run ATE of a reversible dynamic treatment when adjacent-spell carryover is bounded, with Robins point-identification and unrestricted Manski bounds as the two endpoint regimes.

source_refs.claim: Introduces the g-formula for time-varying treatments under sequential exchangeability, the zero-spillover point-identification endpoint.
source_refs.author: Robins

source_refs.claim: Extends assumption-free partial-identification bounds from time-fixed exposures to sustained static and dynamic interventions, emphasizing wide unrestricted bounds.
source_refs.author: Diemer, Shi, Swanson

source_refs.excerpt: retaining the full instrument-memory constraints can only shrink the identified interval
sharp identified set for the long-term ATE is exactly B(P;rho_0,rho_1)

open_problems.why_unsolved: The web literature supplies the two poles but not the bridge: Robins gives point identification under sequential exchangeability, while assumption-free dynamic bounds intentionally leave all latent histories unrestricted. The prior dynamic-IV and surrogate artifacts show the same unresolved shape internally: history retention and bounded transition radii can tighten intervals, but the sharp finite-history or long-run endpoint formula and its attainability proof were not completed.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A theorem giving closed-form lower and upper nested g-formulas indexed by the carryover bound, proving monotone nesting, zero-bound collapse to the Robins formula, infinite-bound collapse to Manski-style bounds, and sharp attainability.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend absorbing-treatment event-study or dynamic-panel identification formulas to reversible treatment paths with repeated on-off spells and bounded carryover contamination.

source_refs.claim: Studies non-binary, non-absorbing panel treatments with treatment lags, but under a parallel-trends design rather than sharp partial identification.
source_refs.author: de Chaisemartin and d'Haultfoeuille

source_refs.claim: Shows contamination of event-study coefficients in staggered absorbing adoption when treatment effects are heterogeneous across periods.
source_refs.author: Sun and Abraham

source_refs.claim: Develops group-time ATT identification for multiple-period DID in staggered adoption settings, relying on adoption-date state compression.
source_refs.author: Callaway and Sant'Anna

source_refs.excerpt: Treatment is absorbing, cohort shares pi_g are fixed

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published DID/event-study results and the in-repo event-study artifacts compress treatment histories to absorbing adoption cohorts. Reversals destroy that state compression: a unit can carry effects from a previous spell, leave treatment, and re-enter, so the missing object is not another event-time contrast but a state space over recent histories with carryover restrictions.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A reversible event-history identification theorem or sharp interval formula that nests the absorbing event-study case and states exactly how spell starts, spell endings, and bounded carryover enter the identified set.

open_problems.open_problem: Construct extremal joint laws proving sharpness of bounded-carryover dynamic-panel bounds for each lower and upper face of the identified set.

source_refs.claim: Provides sharp bounds for average and quantile effects in nonseparable panel models and dynamic bounds for state dependence under time-homogeneity assumptions.
source_refs.author: Chernozhukov, Fernandez-Val, Hahn, Newey

source_refs.claim: Gives sharp identifiable bounds for potential-outcome distributions in panel data with dynamic treatment decisions using stationarity restrictions.
source_refs.author: Lee and Shin

source_refs.excerpt: needs an explicit T=2 witness or an algebraic proof that no such witness exists
Replace the x_d interval with the exact 2x2 coupling bounds

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing panel partial-identification papers prove sharpness for stationarity or nonseparable-panel restrictions, not for scalar bounded spillovers across reversible treatment spells. The prior artifacts make the sharpness gap explicit: endpoint formulas were blocked by missing finite witnesses and by missing exact coupling constraints for latent completions.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: For every endpoint face, a pair of observed-law-compatible latent joint distributions that satisfy the carryover inequalities and attain the proposed lower or upper g-formula bound.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove sharp bounded-anticipation DID intervals when the same latent anticipation or spillover coordinate enters multiple cohort-period contrasts, so coefficients are pooled before support-function absolute values are taken.

source_refs.excerpt: when the same anticipation coordinate enters multiple cohort-period contrasts
sharpness depends on the variation-independent anticipation-box assumption

open_problems.why_unsolved: The support-function algebra is already visible in the prior proposal, but the proof depends on whether latent anticipation or spillover distortions can be varied independently while preserving the observed panel law. The reviewer specifically asked for the compatibility lemma or example family that would turn the scope assumption into a sharpness proof.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp support-function interval theorem for ATT aggregates with either a compatibility lemma proving independent pasteability of shared coordinates or a counterexample giving the smaller compatible interval.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive the bounded-defier retained-memory threshold at which dynamic IV memory gains disappear, allowing hidden response-type compatibility to add faces beyond a simple prism formula.

source_refs.excerpt: There is an observable threshold kappa^dagger_b(P,e,x)>0
second-stage response-type compatibility adds facets to the prism
Define kappa^dagger over the full endpoint-optimal face

open_problems.why_unsolved: The prior artifact conjectures a scalar threshold for when memory stops improving dynamic IV bounds, but the reviewer-flagged LP degeneracy means a single optimizer is not enough. Compatibility between second-stage response types may project the feasible set to a smaller polytope than the displayed coordinatewise prism.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A parametric LP projection theorem giving all endpoint faces as functions of the defier budget kappa, with extremal laws and a zero-defier reduction to the retained-memory parent bound.

open_problems.open_problem: Define a long-run ATE for reversible bounded-memory treatment regimes and characterize when finite-horizon dynamic treatment formulas converge to a stationary or Cesaro-limit identified set.

source_refs.claim: Identifies average potential outcomes for finite treatment sequences and emphasizes dynamic complications such as nonstationarity, state dependence, and timing.
source_refs.author: Han

source_refs.claim: Develops sharp bounds for structural dynamic discrete-choice counterfactuals with scalar welfare endpoints computed through constrained optimization.
source_refs.author: Kalouptsidi, Kitamura, Lima, Souza-Rodrigues

source_refs.claim: Provides computational identification methods for treatment-effect policy evaluation with unobserved heterogeneity rather than closed-form long-run reversible-spell formulas.
source_refs.author: Han and Yang

open_problems.why_unsolved: Finite-horizon dynamic treatment identification does not by itself define the long-run target once reversible treatment histories can keep interacting across adjacent spells. Structural and computational partial-identification approaches show how to bound dynamic counterfactuals, but they do not state analytic conditions under which the horizon-normalized bounds converge or remain closed-form in the spillover radius.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A stationary-limit or Cesaro-limit theorem giving the long-run identified interval as the limit of finite-horizon nested g-formula bounds, including conditions for existence, interchange of limits and extrema, and failure examples.

literature_map:
Robins (1986) is the zero-spillover point-identification anchor: the g-formula identifies dynamic intervention means under sequential exchangeability, positivity, and consistency.
Murphy (2003) and the dynamic treatment-regime literature refine optimal regimes but largely retain point-identifying longitudinal assumptions.
Han (2021, Journal of Econometrics) studies nonparametric identification of finite dynamic treatment sequences and highlights nonstationarity, state dependence, timing, and initial-condition complications.
Han (2024, JASA) studies partial welfare ordering for optimal dynamic regimes using binary instruments; no exact web hit was found for the named Han-Yao-Hsu (2024) string.
Heckman, Humphries, and Veramendi (2016, Journal of Econometrics) separate direct effects from continuation values in multistage choices, which is relevant when reversible spells interact.
Sun and Abraham (2021) and Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) solve major absorbing-treatment event-study and DID identification problems, but their state variable is adoption timing.
de Chaisemartin and d'Haultfoeuille (2024, Review of Economics and Statistics) explicitly allow non-absorbing treatments and lags, but under parallel-trends structure rather than sharp partial identification.
Imai, Kim, and Wang (2023) and Liu, Wang, and Xu (2025) show practical reversible-panel methods using treatment-history matching, imputation, and no-carryover diagnostics.
Chernozhukov, Fernandez-Val, Hahn, and Newey (2013, Econometrica) and Lee and Shin (2016, JBES) provide sharp panel bounds with dynamic decisions or state dependence, but not scalar bounded spillovers between reversible spells.
Diemer, Shi, and Swanson (2024, Epidemiology) provide assumption-free dynamic sustained-intervention bounds, showing the unrestricted endpoint can be very wide.
Kalouptsidi, Kitamura, Lima, Souza-Rodrigues (2020, NBER) and Han and Yang (2024, Journal of Econometrics) represent computational partial-identification approaches rather than closed-form spillover-indexed formulas.
The live gap is a reversible-regime, bounded-carryover sharp partial-identification result that analytically connects Robins point identification to unrestricted Manski-style bounds and includes sharp extremal completions.
prior_proposal_map:
The direct qid pid_reversible_dynamic_spillover has only state/pipeline scaffolding, not a proposal or review artifact.
Closest dynamic partial-ID signal is pid_dynamic_iv_compliance v2/v3: memory-retaining dynamic IV bounds, bounded-defier thresholds, and explicit reviewer demands for endpoint-face witnesses.
Closest bounded-spillover panel signal is pid_did_anticipation_bounded: sharp support intervals under bounded anticipation with shared-coordinate aggregation and a reviewer-prescribed compatibility lemma.
Event-study artifacts are explicitly absorbing-treatment designs, leaving reversible or non-absorbing spell histories unmodeled.
Continuous-treatment DID artifacts contain support-gap sharp-bound problems and dynamic contamination/equivalence concerns, but not a reversible binary spell g-formula.
Long-term surrogate drift artifacts give a bounded-transition template with zero-bound point-ID, Manski fallback, and missing sharp coupling witnesses.
Network-interference panel artifacts add an adjacent bounded-spillover frontier problem, but it was not selected because the requested topic is temporal reversible-spell carryover rather than cross-unit exposure interference.
